planting.note: Plant bulbs with the top third of the bulb above the soil surface in moist to consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; Moore's crinum is notable among crinums for its strong shade-tolerance — it grows naturally in the moist, shaded, coastal forests of the Eastern Cape. The long, distinctive neck on the bulb (to 12 inches or more in old plants) is a diagnostic feature. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant bulbs (upper third exposed) in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; Moore's crinum is the most shade-tolerant of the commonly grown crinum species and one of the few large, summer-flowering bulbs that genuinely thrives in the deep, dry shade under large trees — making it exceptional value in the shade garden where bold summer flowers with tropical presence are difficult to achieve. The flowers are large, lily-like, pale to medium pink, funnel-shaped funnels opening to 4–5 inches across, borne in umbels of 5–10 on stout scapes, fragrant in the evening. Unlike many crinums that require full sun and hot conditions, Moore's crinum originates in the moist, shaded, humid coastal forest margins of the Eastern Cape where it grows with clivias and other shade-lovers. In the shade garden it provides a refreshingly bold, tropical-scale bulb plant for positions under tree canopy, combining naturally with hostas, clivias, and other shade perennials. The long, distinctive, elongated bulb neck — a diagnostic feature of the species, sometimes reaching 12 inches long in old specimens — should be left exposed above the soil when planting. One of the parent species of Crinum x powellii (with C. bulbispermum).

subcategory: large-flowered South American rain lily bulb

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; large habranthus flowers in summer following rain events or deep irrigation, producing among the largest and most striking flowers in the Habranthus genus — funnel-shaped and held at the characteristic Habranthus 45-degree angle, in deep pink-purple to magenta with a deep maroon-purple central throat marking. One of the boldest and most garden-worthy rain lilies, larger than most zephyranthes species.

directions: Plant bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; large habranthus — sometimes called simply 'the Paraguayan rain lily' — is the boldest and most showy of the commonly available habranthus species, producing flowers substantially larger than either H. tubispathus or most Zephyranthes species: 3-inch wide, deep pink-magenta to lavender-pink funnel-shaped blooms with a distinctive, contrasting, deep maroon-purple stain at the center of the throat — a bold, eye-catching combination of warm pink-purple outside and dark maroon-purple center that gives each flower a dramatic bicolor appearance. Like all habranthus, the flowers are held at a graceful 45-degree angle rather than strictly upright. The bloom response to summer rainfall is prompt and reliable — a deep irrigation or summer thunderstorm triggers mass flowering 5–7 days later, the classic rain lily response. It is native to open grasslands of Paraguay, Uruguay, and northern Argentina, climates with hot, wet summers followed by drier winters, conditions that match the Gulf Coast summer growing season well. Several named cultivars exist, including the popular 'Russell Manning' strain with especially large, rosy-pink flowers. Outstanding naturalized in open, sunny borders where the rain-triggered blooming creates a summer spectacle.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; hoop petticoat daffodil naturalizes freely in thin turf and meadow grass where it can grow undisturbed through the spring and summer to ripen foliage and seed. It is particularly effective mass-planted in grass, where it creates spectacular early spring carpets of the distinctive funnel flowers. Established colonies self-seed freely.

directions: Plant small bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; hoop petticoat daffodil is one of the most charmingly eccentric of all daffodil species — the flower form is virtually the opposite of a typical daffodil: instead of a small cup at the center of large, flat petals, it has an enormous, widely flared, deep funnel-trumpet (the 'petticoat') at the center of tiny, almost vestigial, narrow, thread-like outer petals — the whole flower dominated by the expanded trumpet-funnel that gives it the common name of hoop petticoat. In deep golden-yellow (the typical form) or pale cream-white (var. conspicuus, var. citrinus), the little flared-funnel flowers on short stems create a carpet of unusual, distinctive blooms in early to mid spring that draws immediate attention from anyone who knows daffodils as a class — 'What kind of daffodil is THAT?' is the typical first response. Native to the thin, acidic, peaty, rocky grasslands of Spain, Portugal, and northwest Africa, it naturalizes readily in short, thin turf and meadow grass, returning year after year and self-seeding to extend the colony. Excellent for naturalizing in lawns and wild areas in zones 6–9.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 3 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in part shade to full sun; angel's tears needs excellent drainage and dislikes wet conditions, especially in summer dormancy. It is native to the rocky, thin soils of the Iberian Peninsula. Established plants self-seed freely in suitable well-drained conditions. The triandrus group of hybrid daffodils ('Thalia', 'Ice Wings') derives from this species.

directions: Plant small bulbs in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, gritty soil in part shade to full sun; angel's tears is a miniature daffodil species from Spain and Portugal named for its distinctive flower form — the small, nodding, pure-white to cream-white flowers (1–3 per stem, held at a drooping angle as if bowing) each have reflexed outer petals that sweep backward as in a cyclamen flower, and a small, neat, rounded cup at the center, the whole creating an impression of shy, nodding delicacy that gives rise to the common name. The backward-swept petals and nodding, pendant pose are completely distinctive among commonly grown daffodils; no hybrid can quite replicate the graceful, tearful quality of the species. The flowers appear in early spring, usually before the garden daffodils are fully open, in masses of 1–3 nodding, cream-white flowers per stem. It naturalizes slowly but steadily in very well-drained, rocky or sandy garden soils, building colonies of the quiet, nodding white flowers under light-canopy trees in early spring. The parentage of the important Triandrus hybrid daffodil group — including the beloved white 'Thalia' and 'Ice Wings' — making this species the direct ancestor of some of the finest daffodils in cultivation.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; keeled garlic naturalizes freely from self-sown seed and bulbils produced in the flower heads — it can spread aggressively in favorable well-drained, open garden soils. Subsp. pulchellum (purple-flowered) is more commonly grown ornamentally than the pink-flowered type species. The flowers are attractive to bees and butterflies. Mild garlic scent when foliage is bruised.

directions: Plant small bulbs in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; keeled garlic is a European mountain allium — native to the rocky, subalpine meadows and open woodlands of central and southern Europe from the Alps to the Balkans — that is grown ornamentally for its airy, late-summer flower display: loose, open umbels of nodding to spreading, small, pink to reddish-pink to purple (in subsp. pulchellum) bells on slender stems, the individual florets having a pendulous grace that differs from the tight, spherical pompom-type alliums. The flower heads often contain a mix of open flowers and small bulbils — a propagation strategy that makes keeled garlic a prolific self-spreader in open, well-drained garden soils. The bloom period (July–August in most of Europe) provides ornamental value in the midsummer to late-summer garden at a time when many other bulbs have finished. Subsp. pulchellum, with richer purple-pink flowers on taller, more elegant stems, is the form most widely grown ornamentally and is sometimes sold as a separate entity. Outstanding for naturalizing in gravel gardens, dry rock garden slopes, and sunny border fronts where its self-seeding habit creates naturalistic colonies over time.
